text.title: Interview Method Catalog
text.summary: Quick-reference catalog of six interview methods with decision criteria for when to use each.

rule.statement: Use for any role where past behavior predicts future performance: managerial, cross-functional, customer-facing. Same questions for every candidate at the same level.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent questions between candidates make comparison impossible and introduce evaluator bias toward candidates whose answers happened to match their prepared questions.

rule.statement: Pair technical assessment with behavioral for roles at L3+. Time-box at 2-4 hours max. Provide clear evaluation criteria in writing before the candidate begins. Compensate candidate time for take-home assessments >2 hours.
rule.rationale: Uncompensated multi-hour take-homes create selection bias toward candidates who can afford the unpaid time, systematically disadvantaging caregivers and those working multiple jobs.

list.item: Take-home project: deep evaluation, async, best for senior roles.
Live coding: real-time problem-solving and thinking-aloud signal.
Code review: real-world quality judgment, harder to coach.
System design: senior IC and manager roles.
Pair programming: collaboration signal, requires calibrated interviewer.

rule.statement: Never use "culture fit" as a scoring dimension without defining it in observable, values-based terms. "Culture fit" as an undefined concept is a documented path to homogeneous, legally vulnerable hires.
rule.rationale: Undefined culture-fit scores correlate with protected-class characteristics (background, communication style, school pedigree) and are the leading cause of disparate impact claims in hiring.

rule.statement: Use "Would you rehire them?" as the terminal reference question. Ask about specific behaviors from the candidate's STAR responses when possible, to verify claims.
rule.rationale: "Would you rehire?" forces a binary judgment that correlates more strongly with actual performance than open-ended "what were their strengths?" questions, which produce social desirability bias.

rule.statement: Lock debrief discussion until all scorecards are submitted. Review high-variance competencies first. Require that every "hire" and "no hire" recommendation cite specific evidence from the interview record.
rule.rationale: Without a process lock, the first speaker anchors the group. High-variance competencies need resolution before consensus, not after — otherwise strong signals get averaged away.
